sentences.text: This was the brother of my father and the sister of my mother.
They had two boys.
Before they were taken away to concentration camp, my uncle in Antwerp-- he was married to a Gentile woman-- he adopted them.
Yeah, Judah.
Yeah, he lived with them And they, for some reason, when a Gentile person was married to a Jewish man, they did not bother the Jewish man, as far as I know.
And this is what happened to those two boys.
They were lucky.
They came out alive, because they're still living in Toronto.
One's name is-- they call him Maurice, but it's the same name as Meyer.
But they call him Maurice.
And his younger brother is Jack-- Jacob.
Veffer.
--Because it was a brother of my dad.
His name was Joe, Joe B.
Veffer-- like Johnny, they call him.
Yeah.
And they were lucky enough to come alive-- out alive that way.
Yeah.
I never found out why they didn't-- I don't know the reason why they didn't bother the Jewish fellows that married Gentiles.
But in this case, I know that they didn't bother him.
Yeah.
And to come back to the [NON-ENGLISH] in -94-- in the fall of '94--
Yeah.
That's when we moved, because we had to move because she had-- she had a mental lapse, so to speak.
Yeah, Mrs.
McKinney.
And we didn't feel it safe to stay there.
And Hank Bunker was nice enough to take us in for a few days, so to speak.
But not to know that it was until the end of the war.
And lucky enough, we-- that's where we stayed.
But the--
They stayed with the McKinneys.
Yeah.
They had nowhere else to go.
And I guess she-- I guess she could-- she wasn't bothered with them staying there, I guess.
But we-- when we were with Hank Bunker, I remember the food situation was so bad that we decided that we should go-- the firemen still had a lot of food.
So they decided I was the chosen one to go with them.
From the five to six children, I was the chosen one.
So my mother came up the-- with the idea, well, you shouldn't look too Jewish.
Why don't we dye your hair blonde?
So I became a blonde.
It was not really blonde.
It was like red.
And they figured I won't look so Jewish.
So we set out one day in the middle of the night.
Pete Bunker and myself on the bike.
And the bike had no tires.
It had hose.
Like from a hose, they made a tire.
So I drove on there that he had a three-wheeler.
What that means a three-wheeler that has a space to put stuff on there, in front.
And we went, I would say, at least 200 miles, because we drove for hours and hours to get to the people that we wanted to get to.
We got there.
They didn't want no money.
No, we had some gold with-- my mother's, you know, some rings-- the wedding ring, my father's wedding ring.
They had some other stuff that they saved-- coins.
And they loaded us up with nice bit of meat.
And on my bike, they-- I carried 50 pounds of potatoes.
And we slept the night there.
I was tired.
I haven't been out.
My bones were aching.
I got blisters on my tuchus.
And it wasn't very pleasant.
But hey, we went back also in the middle of the night.
We were good for a while.
And I remember halfway, a patrol of Germans passed us.
And for some reason, a couple of planes arrived, and they bombed them to hell.
They flew them right off the floor.
And we hit a ditch.
Like I said, the bullets didn't have my name on it.
So we got out there pretty good.
I say that really fast, but it seemed like hours.
Sure.
The bullets didn't have my name on it.
We got out of that pretty good.
When we got home, it was a feast.
We had a side of beef and potatoes.
And we were a happy family again, because we had something to eat.
He had-- he was a baker, so we had a little bit more to eat there, because he had bread.
But not really enough, you know?
One day he comes, you know, I'm missing a bread.
Does anybody of yous take it?
I wasn't aware.
Until after the war, my brother Alan says, I can't keep it any longer.
He says, it was me who took the extra bread.
We were so hungry.
But those six months were not really that bad, really.
Because we knew that they landed in Normandy, and they were already in Belgium.
And they knew they were in the south of Holland.
You know, they landed in Nijmegen.
You must have heard of that-- the big war in Nijmegen and Arnhem.
So we says, we couldn't be far away.
And there was one night that I hear thunder.
Is that thunder?
No, no, no, no, no, no.
That's cannons.
Ah.
It's coming.
And this is already spring.
This is April.
Then the best day was when Auntie Fifi went, you know what guys?
You can go outside.
I says, I beg your pardon?
We couldn't believe it.
And she says, yeah.
You're free.
And you have no idea how that felt.
That was in May, 1945.
I'm born 1918.
I had my birthday, and I was a free man.
Yes.
That's what I was.
And when somebody tells you you're free-- the Germans were still there, though.
But we didn't care.
You can't do nothing to me.
War's over.
War is over, we can go out.
We walked, that first day we were out.
I had blisters on my feet.
But I walked.
I walked.
And the whole summer was a holiday, was a feast.
I says to my mother, I said-- she asked me, where are you?
I said, don't matter, Ma.
I'm making up for lost time.
And there is a story I will tell you.
It's not a true story, but it's a story.
I never forget.
There was this bull with his little son is born.
And he goes into the meadow.
And I see myself as the little bull.
And he sees all these cows out there.
And he says, daddy, look at the cows.
I think I'll have myself one of those cows.
He says, son, listen to me.
You take your time, and we do them all.
And I always remember that.
That's what I did.
After the war, I made up for lost time.
And like I said, there's two words-- you say thank you.
Every day you get up in the morning.
And you say thank you.
And when it's bad, then you learn to live with it.
And I teach my kids the same way.
You learn to live.
When something's bad, so you have the courage to live with it.
And then be positive.
There's nothing neg-- there's nothing negative.
During the whole time of the world-- of the war, nobody was negative.
There's no such a thing as negative-- positive, always positive.
And even today, I'm positive.
Oh, yes.
At one time, when we-- when we had to get out, we couldn't stay there.
Also again, before we went to-- that's right.
You're right.
When we-- we didn't go, right away, to Pete Bunker.
We went to the family Snell.
And they lived right near a hotel.
What was the name of the hotel?
I can't remember.
Bosch? ?]
Boss? ?] Something like that.
Boss of Brad
And the Jan Tabak Hotel, right.
And we stayed there for about a month.
But while we were there-- that's true.
That was quite a story, by itself.
Again, the food story was, we we're always out of food.
But we had a good friend in Blariacum.
He had a vegetable wholesale.
So who was suggested to go?
Mipey ?] and [?
Uppy-- ?] that means Max and Alan.
So it was in the middle of the winter.
We took a sleigh.
And I knew my way about-- through the woods, because we used to play there as kids.
And I knew my way to Blariacum.
We walked for a while.
It's about an hour and a half walk.
We walk.
And all of a sudden, I hear a scream-- help!
I say, what the heck is happening?
Where are you, Alan?
And I can't see him.
I don't see him at all.
I walk back.
What happened?
He walked into a hole into the ground, right up to his ne-- up to nose.
I picked him out of there, and he's crying.
I'm going back.
I said, no you ain't.
No way.
You're going with me.
All the way, hang on to my coat, all the way to Blariacum.
We got there-- nice people, beautiful people.
They fed us.
That was a good meal I remember.
They gave us a good plate of soup, and they gave us a good side of beef to eat.
I couldn't eat it all.
But we ate it, anyways.
They gave us some vegetables, potatoes, and we're on our way back.
Quite a walk.
And we got out onto the main road.
Sure enough, there was a patrol and they stopped us.
He got so scared, he ran.
And the guy-- and he's going to shoot him.
Said, whoa, whoa, whoa.
Don't shoot him.
He's got to go to the washroom.
That stopped him.
And by the time I got home, he told my father and mother, they're shooting [?
Maxi. ?] He won't be back.
When they saw me, like the king walked in with the food, naturally.
And like I say, every day was a story.
But this was-- it all had a happy ending, thank god.
Yeah, we forgot that story.
Thank you for reminding me.
But like I said, going back to the-- after the war, we tried to get our store back.
But the guy that looked after the-- my dad's business, he already opened it up for himself.
We couldn't get that store back, for some reason.
My mom got so mad, because the store--
Oh, yeah.
During the war, he was going to-- he was going to keep it for my dad, because my dad founded the business.
And he was going to keep it.
But he got scared, or maybe he wanted the money on his own.
I don't know.
But he says, Mr.
Veffer, I can't keep it anymore, because they may be thinking I'm working for you.
And after the war, we'll straighten out.
But he never did.
My mom got so mad, she got somebody to-- because everything that was in there was ours.
And he had no right to that, because we never sold it to him.
She took everything out.
We cleaned out the whole store.
She says, now there, I got even with you.
But we, from the--
We used it in the store that we opened.
The mayor of the city-- beautiful man.
I can't recall his name.
But I remember, he was this tall man with a mustache.
I remember that.
I see his face.
But I can't remember his name.
He got us, within two days, he got us another store just up the street.
We opened it up.
But it wasn't the same.
It wasn't the same.
People were not the same.
In what way?
Some of them kept stuff from Jewish people.
And they were sorry to see you come back so that-- because they had to give it back.
There was a lot of that going on.
They didn't like to see you come back.
And people were not the same.
I tried to go play soccer with the same people, but I didn't see the same faces.
I didn't feel comfortable.
Couldn't pick-- couldn't put my finger on it, what it was.
But it wasn't the same.
So therefore, I went to play for-- I learned to go to Amsterdam, because most Jewish people that came back from concentration camp came back to Amsterdam.
OK?
And my dad had a friend in the diamond business, so he taught me to be a diamond cutter.
Did you think to go back to school at all at that time?
Yeah, oh yes.
Not me.
Not me.
My brother Johnny was the only one of the school age that went back, and my brother Joe.
But Alan and myself learned how to be a diamond cutter.
My brother Jack went back to school.
But he couldn't cope with it.
Why?
There's no reason.
It just mentally, he wasn't equipped.
And my sister Shelley, she stayed at home, helped my mom.
It wasn't the same.
It was just not the same.
Mind you, I learned the diamond cutting, and we had a good time there because I met a good friend of mine.
And I was not too much home.
I would be on Friday night, and we get together.
And then I didn't come up for air until Monday morning.
Why?
Because we had a good time, because Holland was-- oh, for about a year, it took, until everything got back to normal.
It's very hard to describe how people felt-- even the Gentile people.
Because you were free-- you could do what you want.
You could say what you want.
You could go where you want.
That went on for, oh, the whole summer.
All summer, until right into the winter.
And then things start to finally get back to normal.
It wasn't us who tried.
There was a special organization.
I forgot the name of them.
This-- even today, they try to find Jewish people.
You know what I'm saying?
But what we got the answer of-- no.
And there was a place in Amsterdam that the Jewish people gathered socially.
It was-- It was like a restaurant ballroom.
OK?
And on Sundays and the weekend, the Jewish people would get together.
And you'd be surprised how many people we met there that we didn't know that they came back from concentration camp.
See, everybody got to know that place.
It was called the Ice Breaker.
It's called Ice Breaker-- it-- an English name.
We gave it an English name.
It was owned by a Jewish guy.
And it was very smart, because you felt at home there.
You go there, and you meet people that you haven't seen for a long, long time.
And we used to go there.
We had a lot of places, but on Sundays that's where the whole family, actually, go there and have a good time.
And that's how we all settled down, actually, to what you'd say a normal.
But it wasn't the same.
Like because, like I said, on Friday night when-- when we were liberated-- by the way, there was-- the first guys were the Canadians.
There reason I know, because there was guys from Nova Scotia.
But I didn't know what Nova Scotia, but I knew it was Canadian.
And next door--
My first impression-- they were nice guys.
They-- they liberated us.
They were very friendly.
Very friendly, and had nice uniforms on-- green.
And I remember next door there was an officers' mess.
And one of the guys that were there, there were a couple of Jewish fellas.
And how they found out right away that we were Jewish?
On Friday night, my mother invited them for dinner.
You know, on Friday night.
And one of them was my-- now, my brother-in-law, Marty Stern.
And I guess he took a liking to my sister.
And they got married in Holland, after the war.
Yeah, I cannot go out with a Canadian, she said.
So she asked my mother while we were in hiding, if we get out, can I go out with a Canadian?
We were very strict-- a very strict family.
She said, we are out, you can go out with a Canadian.
When she first went out with a-- the first one she went out, his name was-- that's how I know from Nova Scotia.
The first guy she went out with name-- I don't remember his last name.
Dick, his name was Dick.
And he was from Nova Scotia, because they have it marked on there.
He was a tough-looking guy, but nice.
And he was a guy also that took me on a motorcycle.
At first, and when they came in, that was a big thing.
They came in, the first day.
They rode on the big tanks.
And we saw all the-- all the bad the bad people being picked up-- the quislings, so to speak.
And all of a sudden, somebody says, hey, you better go see.
Your brother Joey got shot.
He got shot?
How could he get shot?
The war is over.
Well, they tried to get a quisling out of the-- out of his house.
He didn't want to come out, and he had a gun.
And he starts shooting.
And it must have ricocheted.
And it hit his back.
Lucky enough, it went in-- it went in and out.
In the meantime, I'm getting there, he's laying in a puddle of blood-- my kid brother, Joey.
So they got him to the hospital, and it worked out OK-- happy ending, happy ending.
But the war was over, and he got shot.
OK?
And your father was told about that and came to find him, I think?
No, no, no.
I found out.
I was out with Joe.
Joe was out with me and Alan.
We were having a good time-- the tanks and everybody there, and the parade, and all that sort of stuff.
But in the meantime, this was going on.
They were rounding up all the bad people.
Like we used to call them the black shirts.
They had black shirts on, and they used to parade like the Germans.
They used to-- but we never, as kids.
They were there before the war, too.
But we never thought any bad of them, because hey, they didn't bother us.
But they were-- they were bad people.
But the best thing I like to remember, though-- we got, we got out free, Scot free.
The only thing I feel bad, that the people-- and I'd like to dedicate this video to the people's life in the concentration camps.
Their lives got wasted.
Their lives got wasted.
I would have liked to see them come back and tell their story.
They didn't come back.
And that was the best part of the story.
And I like this project that Steve Spielberg-- that he donated such a-- his time, and the people that used this TV.
And I hope a new generation will learn from this, and that they should understand that the pain that we went through and that we endured.
And I hope they learn from it.
And they should have peace and love each other.
That's important.
And that's why I like to believe that this-- what I, my story-- will help them to be better people, and that there should be peace on earth, and they should live in peace.
My grandchildren shouldn't go through the [SOBBING] ordeal that we went through.
I think I said enough.
This is enough for me.
Oh, we had-- we used to send them parcels for Christmas, yeah.
There's one thing that came nice out of this world, out of this war-- that my mother wrote a book.
This is your life, and this is 1,000 days in hiding.
That's the nice part.
And a relative of hers was a secretary to Ralph Edwards.
That's the nice part that came out of this.
And he had us on his show.
That was, that was a nice part.
We were there for three days.
And they treated us like god.
That was beautiful.
Oh, yeah.
Thanks for reminding me.
I get a little bit emotional, I'm sorry.
We left in '47.
My mother made up her mind, because my dad was didn't-- he was homesick for my sister.
So a relative of my brother-in-law, Marty, runs a flower shop in Toronto.
And he sponsored them.
So my dad was the first one to go over, and then he sponsored us because he was here, so he could sponsor us.
Oh, yeah.
She had married Marty right after the war, because they got married.
That was nice.
That was the only Jewish wedding I ever seen, and being there in Holland, to be quite honest with you.
And we went first class on the Veendam, first class.
That was nine days on the boat.
And my mother never saw me.
What do you do?
I says, well, I'll tell you.
I meet up with some nice people.
Who are these people?
They're nice people, Ma.
She, well, what can you do on a, on a boat?
I said, Ma, trust me.
I'm having a good time.
And the only guy-- the one person that knew that I had a good time was a steward.
He kept me-- he kept me, feeding eggnog's in the morning.
So I had a good time.
That was a nice trip.
We got to Toronto.
It was bitter cold, bitter cold.
I remember, my brother-in-law picked us up from Union Station, and we got outside.
I says, my god.
This is cold.
And all I see is snow.
I says to my mother, Ma, what kind of a country are we coming to?
Eskimos?
Huh?
No, no, no, my brother-in-law says.
You'll be OK.
It was on a Sunday that he picked us up, so everything was closed-- conservative, Toronto-- everything locked.
She said, what am I-- I was homesick.
I was homesick for about a year.
But we fit in pretty quick.
After a while I got to know a few people-- nice, nice fellas.
They introduced me to B'nai B'rith.
I joined B'nai B'rith.
And they were the ones introduced me to baseball, football, hockey.
I liked hockey.
We never played hockey back home.
And they treated me nicely.
We went to the baseball game, and we went to a place in the summertime.
They took us to Crystal Beach.
Yeah, that was the time where we hung out.
Crystal Beach-- we go on a Friday night, and don't come up for air until Monday.
Yeah, that was a nice-- that's where we took-- we went with a bunch of guys to-- they always fit-- I probably fit in faster than anybody, because I met up with a nice bunch of guys.
I mean, they had a habit of-- the Canadians, they used to call us greener, or they called us DP.
And the guy calls me DP.
I say, hey, listen.
I'm not a displaced person.
I came from Holland.
I was born there, and I immigrated to Canada.
And I'm a Canadian, not a DP.
Oh, excuse me.
All right.
So we went-- and that's how I got through that, because a lot of them treated us like-- well, what are you doing here?
You know?
I said, well, this is my country now.
How did you learn English?
How did I learn English?
I didn't go to night school.
But I learned English, because I mixed with the people that I know, and I read the paper.
And you have to learn English because if you don't speak English, how are you going to get along with the girls?
You know, they fix me up with blind dates.
And if you sit there with your mouth full and don't talk-- but I managed-- I managed.
I had a good time.
And that's how I met my wife.
I belonged to YMHA, and they had these dances on Sunday night-- call it fate.
I'm a fatalist.
I come back to the same thing, I tell you.
If it happens, it happens to you.
Because that particular night-- there was no TV in those days.
We listened to the hockey game Sunday night, which is already 9:30.
A friend phones me, you want to go to the YMHA?
No, I'm in my pajamas.
You go.
No, I don't want to go alone.
Come on, put your clothes on.
Sure enough, he picked me up at 10:00.
And those days they are finished at 12:00.
I walked in the door.
And we walked in, and I literally tripped over a person.
Who is that person?
My wife.
And that's how I met my wife, at YMHA.
She was here on a holiday.
She had some relatives here.
She was from?
Montreal.
Yeah, born in Montreal.
Yeah.
And we got married in January, 1951.
Pascal, Sylvia Pascal.
Nice family, nice family-- she had a lovely mom, a lovely mom, may she rest in peace.
A nice father, brother, sisters, and--
Yeah.
